g.text: After io_uring_setup() + mmap() — Three Shared Memory Regions
Size: 16 bytes (1 cache line for 4 CQEs)
SQ head | Kernel | App | Kernel consumes SQEs
SQ tail | App | Kernel | App publishes SQEs
CQ head | App | Kernel | App consumes CQEs
CQ tail | Kernel | App | Kernel publishes CQEs
Each side ONLY writes indices it owns — no synchronization needed

ul.li: sq_head: Kernel consumes SQEs from here
array[]: Maps logical index → physical SQE slot

div.p: The trap: x86 has strong memory ordering, so developers assume barriers aren't needed. The reality: The compiler can reorder independent memory writes! c // DANGEROUS: compiler may reorder! sqes[idx].opcode = IORING_OP_READ; // Write SQE data *sq_tail = new_tail; // Publish tail // If kernel reads new_tail before SQE is written → GARBAGE!
The solution: Use smp_store_release() when publishing, smp_load_acquire() when consuming.

ul.li: Store-release: Ensures all prior writes complete before the store becomes visible

Load-acquire: Ensures all subsequent reads see data after the load completes On x86, these compile to compiler barriers (asm volatile("" ::: "memory")). On ARM, they compile to actual dmb ish instructions.

g.text: struct io_uring_sqe *sqes = mmap(NULL, sqes_size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE,

g.text: SQ head, CQ tail (kernel writes, app reads)
